name: autonomous-agent-patterns
description: Design patterns for building autonomous coding agents. Covers tool integration, permission systems, browser automation, and human-in-the-loop workflows. Use when building AI agents, designing tool APIs, implementing permission systems, or creating autonomous coding assistants.
capabilities: Design agent loop architecture with think-decide-act-observe cycles
Implement tool schemas and essential agent tools (read, write, edit files)
Build permission and approval systems with risk-based levels
Create sandboxed execution environments for command safety
Implement browser automation with visual element detection
Manage context injection and checkpoint/resume patterns
author: sickn33
securitySummary: This skill is a reference documentation resource containing code examples for building autonomous agents. All detected patterns (external_commands, network, filesystem, env_access) are documentation examples demonstrating legitimate agent patterns, not actual executable malicious code. The skill does not contain prompt injection attempts or malicious intent. All findings are false positives - the skill is educational content about agent architecture.
valueStatement: Learn proven design patterns for creating autonomous coding agents with proper tool integration, permission systems, and safety controls. Essential for developers building AI-powered development tools.
limitations: This is a reference documentation skill, not a functional tool
Requires implementation in your own codebase - no runtime provided
Does not include actual AI model integration - only patterns
Browser automation examples require Playwright/Puppeteer setup

useCases.title: Build a Claude Code extension
useCases.description: Create a VS Code extension or CLI tool that uses Claude for autonomous code editing with proper permission controls.

useCases.targetUser: Framework developers creating AI agent platforms
useCases.title: Design secure agent APIs
useCases.description: Implement tool calling APIs with permission levels, approval dialogs, and sandboxing for AI agent frameworks.

useCases.title: Add browser automation to agents
useCases.description: Enable your AI agent to interact with web applications through visual element detection and automation.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Agent Loop
promptTemplates.prompt: Show me how to implement a basic agent loop in Python that uses an LLM to decide when to use tools versus return a final answer.
promptTemplates.scenario: Learning the fundamentals of agent architecture

promptTemplates.title: Permission System
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a permission system for an AI agent with different levels: auto-approve for safe operations, ask-once for moderate risk, ask-each-time for dangerous commands, and never-allow for blocked operations.
promptTemplates.scenario: Implementing safety controls in agent tools

promptTemplates.title: Sandboxed Execution
promptTemplates.prompt: Write a Python class that safely executes shell commands in a sandboxed environment, restricting which commands can run and limiting filesystem access to a workspace directory.
promptTemplates.scenario: Building secure command execution for agents

promptTemplates.title: MCP Tool Discovery
promptTemplates.prompt: Show how to implement dynamic MCP server connection that discovers available tools and allows creating new tools at runtime based on user descriptions.

bestPractices: Always implement permission levels - auto-approve safe read operations but require approval for file writes and command execution
Use sandboxed execution environments to restrict which commands and filesystem paths are accessible
Design tools with clear JSON schemas so the LLM understands what each tool does and its parameters
antiPatterns: Allowing unrestricted command execution without permission checks or sandboxing
Giving agents unlimited context without proper context management and size limits
Implementing tools with vague descriptions that make it unclear what the tool does

faq.question: Is this skill ready to use or do I need to implement it?
faq.answer: This is a reference documentation skill with code examples. You need to implement the patterns in your own codebase. It teaches you how to build autonomous agents, it is not a functioning agent itself.

faq.question: Which AI models work with these patterns?
faq.answer: These patterns work with any LLM that supports tool calling, including Claude, GPT-4, and other models. The patterns are model-agnostic.

faq.question: Do I need special permissions to run these agent patterns?
faq.answer: The patterns include their own permission systems. You do not need special permissions to study or implement them. The skill itself does not execute any code.

faq.question: Can these patterns damage my system?
faq.answer: The patterns include safety features like sandboxing and permission levels. However, when implementing them, you are responsible for proper security configuration. Always restrict dangerous commands and limit filesystem access.

faq.question: How do I get started with building an agent?
faq.answer: Start with the Core Agent Architecture section to understand the think-decide-act-observe loop. Then implement basic tools from section 2. Add permission controls from section 3 before enabling any dangerous operations.

faq.question: What is MCP and how does it relate to these patterns?
faq.answer: MCP (Model Context Protocol) is a standard for connecting AI agents to external tools and data sources. Section 6 shows how to implement dynamic tool discovery using MCP, allowing agents to find and use tools automatically.
